case_name: Kesavananda Bharati v State of Kerala
citation: Kesavananda Bharati v State of Kerala, AIR 1973 SC 1461
text: KESAVANANDA BHARATI v STATE OF KERALA (1973). COURT: Supreme Court of India. FACTS: His Holiness Kesavananda Bharati, the head of a Hindu mutt (religious institution), challenged the Kerala Land Reforms Act, which imposed restrictions on the management and use of religious property. The case was heard by a 13-judge Constitutional Bench, the largest ever assembled by the Supreme Court of India. LEGAL ISSUES: Whether Parliament's power to amend the Constitution under Article 368 is unlimited, or whether there are implied limitations on the amending power. HOLDING: The Supreme Court held, by a 7-6 majority, that while Parliament has wide powers to amend the Constitution, it cannot alter the BASIC STRUCTURE of the Constitution. The basic structure includes: the supremacy of the Constitution, the republican and democratic form of government, the secular character of the Constitution, the separation of powers, and the federal character of the Constitution. REASONING: The majority held that the power of amendment under Article 368 does not include the power to destroy or abrogate the basic features of the Constitution. An amendment that abrogates the basic structure is beyond Parliament's constituent power and is void. The basic structure doctrine serves as a judicial check on the amendment power. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established the BASIC STRUCTURE DOCTRINE — one of the most important constitutional law doctrines in the world. (2) Placed implied limitations on constitutional amendment power. (3) The case has been followed in Bangladesh, Pakistan, and other common law jurisdictions. (4) Remains the foundational case in Indian constitutional law.

case_name: Maneka Gandhi v Union of India
citation: Maneka Gandhi v Union of India, AIR 1978 SC 597
text: MANEKA GANDHI v UNION OF INDIA (1978). COURT: Supreme Court of India. FACTS: Maneka Gandhi's passport was impounded by the government without giving her any reason or opportunity to be heard. She challenged the action under Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether the right to travel abroad is part of the right to personal liberty under Article 21. (2) Whether Article 21 only requires a 'procedure established by law' or whether the procedure must also be fair, just, and reasonable. HOLDING: The Supreme Court held that the right to travel abroad is part of the right to personal liberty under Article 21. Further, the procedure established by law under Article 21 must satisfy the requirements of natural justice — it must be fair, just, and reasonable, not arbitrary, fanciful, or oppressive. REASONING: The Court expanded the interpretation of Article 21 from A.K. Gopalan v State of Madras (1950). Justice P.N. Bhagwati held that Articles 14, 19, and 21 are not mutually exclusive watertight compartments but are interconnected. Any law depriving a person of personal liberty must satisfy the test of reasonableness under Article 14 and the freedoms guaranteed under Article 19. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Expanded the scope of Article 21 RIGHT TO LIFE to include DUE PROCESS. (2) Established that fundamental rights are interconnected. (3) One of the most important expansions of fundamental rights in Indian constitutional history. (4) Laid the groundwork for subsequent expansions of Article 21 to include the right to livelihood, right to education, right to privacy, and others.

citation: Vishaka v State of Rajasthan, AIR 1997 SC 3011
text: VISHAKA v STATE OF RAJASTHAN (1997). COURT: Supreme Court of India. FACTS: Bhanwari Devi, a social worker in Rajasthan, was gang-raped as retaliation for her efforts to stop child marriage. The acquittal of the accused by the trial court prompted a public interest litigation by Vishaka and other women's groups challenging the absence of legislation addressing sexual harassment at the workplace. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether sexual harassment at the workplace violates fundamental rights under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21. (2) In the absence of legislation, what guidelines should apply. HOLDING: The Supreme Court held that sexual harassment at the workplace violates fundamental rights and laid down detailed guidelines (the 'Vishaka Guidelines') to be followed until legislation was enacted. REASONING: The Court drew upon CEDAW (Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women) and international norms to fill the legislative vacuum. The Court exercised its power under Article 32 and Article 141 to declare the law. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) The VISHAKA GUIDELINES served as binding law for 16 years until the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 was enacted. (2) Landmark in using international conventions to interpret fundamental rights. (3) Foundational case for workplace safety and gender justice in India.

case_name: Navtej Singh Johar v Union of India
citation: Navtej Singh Johar v Union of India, (2018) 10 SCC 1
text: NAVTEJ SINGH JOHAR v UNION OF INDIA (2018). COURT: Supreme Court of India. FACTS: Navtej Singh Johar and others challenged Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, which criminalized consensual sexual acts 'against the order of nature,' as violating the fundamental rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution. HOLDING: A five-judge Constitution Bench unanimously held that Section 377 was unconstitutional insofar as it criminalized consensual sexual conduct between adults. REASONING: The Court held that sexual orientation is a natural and inherent aspect of identity protected by the right to privacy (Article 21), equality (Article 14), non-discrimination (Article 15), and freedom of expression (Article 19). Consensual sexual acts between adults in private cannot be criminalized as they fall within the zone of privacy and dignity. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Decriminalized homosexuality in India. (2) Landmark in LGBTQ+ rights in India. (3) Recognized SEXUAL ORIENTATION as a protected characteristic under the Constitution. (4) Built upon the right to privacy established in K.S. Puttaswamy v Union of India (2017).

case_name: K.S. Puttaswamy v Union of India (Right to Privacy)
citation: K.S. Puttaswamy v Union of India, (2017) 10 SCC 1
text: K.S. PUTTASWAMY v UNION OF INDIA (2017). COURT: Supreme Court of India. A nine-judge Constitution Bench unanimously held that the RIGHT TO PRIVACY is a fundamental right protected under Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution. The Court overruled M.P. Sharma v Satish Chandra (1954) and Kharak Singh v State of UP (1963) to the extent they held that privacy is not a fundamental right. The Court held that privacy includes: informational privacy, the privacy of choice, and bodily integrity. Any interference with privacy must satisfy the tests of legality, legitimate aim, proportionality, and procedural safeguards. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established the RIGHT TO PRIVACY as a fundamental right. (2) One of the largest benches (9 judges) to decide a constitutional question. (3) Foundation for subsequent cases on LGBTQ+ rights, data protection, and bodily autonomy.

citation: Golaknath v State of Punjab, AIR 1967 SC 1643
text: GOLAKNATH v STATE OF PUNJAB (1967). COURT: Supreme Court of India. An 11-judge bench held that Parliament cannot amend Part III (Fundamental Rights) of the Constitution. The Court treated constitutional amendments as 'law' under Article 13 and held that any amendment that abrogated fundamental rights would be void. This decision was later effectively modified by the 24th Amendment and superseded by Kesavananda Bharati (1973). SIGNIFICANCE: The case initiated the debate about the limits of amendment power that ultimately led to the basic structure doctrine in Kesavananda Bharati.

case_name: S.R. Bommai v Union of India
citation: S.R. Bommai v Union of India, AIR 1994 SC 1918
text: S.R. BOMMAI v UNION OF INDIA (1994). COURT: Supreme Court of India. FACTS: Multiple state governments were dismissed by the President under Article 356 (President's Rule). The dismissed Chief Ministers challenged the dismissals. HOLDING: A nine-judge bench held that the President's power under Article 356 is subject to judicial review. The Court laid down guidelines for the exercise of Article 356, holding that secularism and federalism are basic features of the Constitution. The proclamation under Article 356 must be based on relevant material and is subject to judicial scrutiny. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established that PRESIDENT'S RULE is subject to judicial review. (2) Affirmed SECULARISM and FEDERALISM as basic features. (3) Curtailed misuse of Article 356 for political purposes. (4) Key case on Indian federalism.

case_name: M.C. Mehta v Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak Case)
citation: M.C. Mehta v Union of India, AIR 1987 SC 1086
text: M.C. MEHTA v UNION OF INDIA (Oleum Gas Leak Case, 1987). COURT: Supreme Court of India. FACTS: Oleum gas leaked from the Shriram Industries plant in Delhi, causing illness and one death. The case arose in the aftermath of the Bhopal Gas Tragedy. HOLDING: The Supreme Court established the principle of ABSOLUTE LIABILITY — an enterprise engaged in inherently dangerous or hazardous activities owes an absolute duty to the community to ensure that no harm results. This liability is not subject to any of the exceptions available under the rule in Rylands v Fletcher. REASONING: Justice P.N. Bhagwati held that the rule in Rylands v Fletcher (English law) was inadequate for modern Indian conditions. Indian law must develop its own principles to meet the needs of a developing society facing industrial hazards. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established the doctrine of ABSOLUTE LIABILITY in Indian environmental law, going beyond the English strict liability rule. (2) Pioneer in developing indigenous Indian legal principles. (3) Foundation for Indian environmental jurisprudence.
